content.text: is the structured sequence of checks that a team executes after a build is declared a release candidate and before any real user traffic reaches the new version. For GenAI services the bar is higher than for conventional software: the model itself is a probabilistic component whose failure modes — hallucinations, latency spikes from long output sequences, unexpected cost overruns, safety-filter misclassifications — cannot be caught by type-checking or unit tests alone. Verification must be empirical: you run the service under realistic conditions and measure.

A pre-launch verification process answers four questions before any gatekeeper signs off. Does the service start and stay healthy? Does the production environment match the environment where the build was tested? Do all critical user journeys succeed end-to-end? Does the new version behave as well as the current production baseline when given a small slice of real traffic? Answering all four requires four distinct verification stages: smoke tests, staging parity checks, integration suite execution, and a canary soak.

Skipping or shortcutting pre-launch verification for GenAI services is disproportionately expensive. Unlike a stateless REST endpoint where a bad deploy causes a spike in 500s that is quickly visible and rolled back, a broken GenAI service can silently return plausible-sounding but incorrect answers, exceed token budgets, or invoke downstream tools with malformed arguments — degrading user trust before any alert fires. The combination of non-determinism, latency sensitivity (p99 can be 10x p50 for long-output models), and per-request cost makes early verification the cheapest possible form of incident prevention.
— secrets missing from prod, wrong model endpoint URLs, network ACLs blocking the model provider — before users see them.
— the canary pass/fail threshold gives a quantitative, team-agreed reason to abort rather than a judgment call under pressure.
— integration tests can assert that the token cap, rate-limiter, and budget alert are all wired up before full rollout multiplies per-request cost across all users.
— test results linked to the launch ticket form the evidence base for a Go/No-Go forum and for post-incident analysis if a problem appears later.
— running verification together forces SRE, engineering, QA, and PM to agree on what passing means before the pressure of launch day.

— a full four-stage pipeline with a 30-minute canary soak adds 60–90 minutes to every deploy. For low-risk patches this feels excessive. The answer is not to skip stages but to maintain fast-path variants (abbreviated smoke test only) for trivial config-only changes where the risk profile is provably different.

— routing 5% of production traffic through the new version during the soak period means paying for model calls that will not be kept if the canary rolls back. For high-traffic, expensive-model services this is a real budget line item.

— staging traffic volumes, user mix, and upstream service versions always diverge from production over time. The parity check slows this drift but cannot eliminate it; some classes of production-only failures will only be caught by the canary stage.
— GenAI output quality cannot be verified with exact-match assertions. Integration tests must use model-graded evaluation or heuristic scoring, both of which have false-negative rates.

Verification runs as a sequential pipeline of four stages, each with a binary pass/fail gate. A failure at any stage stops the pipeline immediately; the team diagnoses and fixes before resuming from the failed stage (not from the beginning, unless the fix touches earlier-stage concerns).

Run against the production environment within two minutes of deploy. Assert that every service dependency (health endpoint, model provider, auth, database, queue consumers) responds. A failure here means the deploy itself did not succeed and there is no point proceeding.

A config diff tool compares staging and production across model version, network topology, feature flags, data-masking rules, and environment variables. The target is zero unexplained differences. Differences found here are fixed or explicitly acknowledged before proceeding.
End-to-end happy-path flows, adversarial prompt suite, output-filter validation, latency SLO assertions, and cost-cap trigger tests all run. Owner: Eng + QA. Target: 100% pass rate.

1–5% of real production traffic is routed to the new version for a minimum of 30 minutes. Error rate, p99 latency, cost per request, and sampled output quality are compared against the shadow baseline. Rollback is automatic if any metric exceeds its threshold.
all four stages green, results stored in the launch ticket, Go/No-Go forum sign-off recorded.

attrs.alt: Pre-launch verification pipeline: Stage 1 Smoke Tests, Stage 2 Staging Parity, Stage 3 Integration Suite, Stage 4 Canary Validation, with Definition of Done and Rollback Decision Tree

content.text: Run the full four-stage pipeline for every deploy that changes model version, system prompt, tool definitions, output filtering logic, or any infrastructure component such as load balancer rules, network policies, or secret rotation. For documentation-only changes, a smoke test only is sufficient. For config changes that toggle a feature flag already tested in staging, run stages 1 and 2.

Do not run the canary stage when you have fewer than roughly 200 requests per hour in production — the sample is too small for statistically meaningful comparison against baseline. In that case, extend the integration suite with a broader set of synthetic requests and treat those results as your canary proxy.

Wire the four stages into your CI/CD pipeline as post-deploy jobs. Each stage exits non-zero on failure, stopping the pipeline. Store results as build artifacts and post a summary to the launch tracking ticket automatically.

# smoke_tests.py - Stage 1: Smoke test suite for a GenAI service
# Run with: pytest smoke_tests.py -v --timeout=120

import time
import pytest
import httpx
import os

BASE_URL = os.environ["SERVICE_BASE_URL"]
API_KEY = os.environ["SERVICE_API_KEY"]
MODEL_PROBE_PROMPT = "Reply with exactly the word READY and nothing else."


@pytest.fixture(scope="session")
def client():
    return httpx.Client(
        base_url=BASE_URL,
        headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {API_KEY}"},
        timeout=30.0,
    )


def test_health_endpoint_returns_200(client):
    """Service health endpoint must return 200 within 5 seconds."""
    start = time.monotonic()
    resp = client.get("/health")
    elapsed = time.monotonic() - start
    assert resp.status_code == 200, f"Health check failed: {resp.status_code}"
    assert elapsed < 5.0, f"Health check too slow: {elapsed:.1f}s"
    data = resp.json()
    assert data.get("status") == "ok", f"Unexpected health body: {data}"


def test_model_probe_responds(client):
    """The model endpoint must return a non-empty response to a minimal prompt."""
    resp = client.post("/v1/chat", json={
        "messages": [{"role": "user", "content": MODEL_PROBE_PROMPT}],
        "max_tokens": 10,
    })
    assert resp.status_code == 200, f"Model probe failed: {resp.status_code} {resp.text}"
    body = resp.json()
    content = body["choices"][0]["message"]["content"]
    assert content.strip(), "Model returned empty content"


def test_auth_rejects_invalid_token():
    """Requests with a bad token must receive 401, not 500."""
    bad_client = httpx.Client(
        base_url=BASE_URL,
        headers={"Authorization": "Bearer invalid-token-000"},
        timeout=10.0,
    )
    resp = bad_client.get("/health")
    assert resp.status_code == 401, f"Expected 401, got {resp.status_code}"


def test_db_connection_via_healthz(client):
    """Deep health endpoint confirms database connectivity."""
    resp = client.get("/healthz/deep")
    assert resp.status_code == 200
    checks = resp.json().get("checks", {})
    assert checks.get("database") == "ok", f"DB check failed: {checks}"


def test_queue_consumers_running(client):
    """Queue consumer health must report at least one active consumer."""
    resp = client.get("/healthz/deep")
    assert resp.status_code == 200
    checks = resp.json().get("checks", {})
    assert checks.get("queue_consumers") == "ok", (
        f"Queue consumers not running: {checks}"
    )
The following script checks error rate and p99 latency of a canary pod against the baseline and exits non-zero if any metric breaches its threshold:

# canary_validate.py - Stage 4: Compare canary metrics against baseline
# Usage: python canary_validate.py --window-minutes 30 --error-rate-threshold 0.10

import argparse
import sys
import httpx


def fetch_prometheus_scalar(prom_url: str, query: str) -> float:
    """Execute an instant Prometheus query and return the scalar result."""
    resp = httpx.get(f"{prom_url}/api/v1/query", params={"query": query}, timeout=15)
    resp.raise_for_status()
    data = resp.json()["data"]["result"]
    if not data:
        return 0.0
    return float(data[0]["value"][1])


def main():
    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser()
    parser.add_argument("--prom-url", default="http://prometheus.internal:9090")
    parser.add_argument("--window-minutes", type=int, default=30)
    parser.add_argument("--error-rate-threshold", type=float, default=0.10,
                        help="Max allowed error rate delta vs baseline (0.10 = 10pp)")
    parser.add_argument("--latency-p99-threshold-ms", type=float, default=4000)
    args = parser.parse_args()

    window = f"{args.window_minutes}m"
    failures = []

    # Error rate comparison
    canary_err = fetch_prometheus_scalar(
        args.prom_url,
        f'sum(rate(http_requests_total{{job="genai-canary",status=~"5.."}[{window}]))'
        f' / sum(rate(http_requests_total{{job="genai-canary"}}[{window}]))',
    )
    baseline_err = fetch_prometheus_scalar(
        args.prom_url,
        f'sum(rate(http_requests_total{{job="genai-stable",status=~"5.."}[{window}]))'
        f' / sum(rate(http_requests_total{{job="genai-stable"}}[{window}]))',
    )
    err_delta = canary_err - baseline_err
    print(f"Error rate  - canary: {canary_err:.3%}  baseline: {baseline_err:.3%}  delta: {err_delta:+.3%}")
    if err_delta > args.error_rate_threshold:
        failures.append(
            f"ERROR RATE exceeded threshold: delta {err_delta:.3%} > {args.error_rate_threshold:.3%}"
        )

    # p99 latency comparison
    canary_p99 = fetch_prometheus_scalar(
        args.prom_url,
        f'histogram_quantile(0.99, rate(http_request_duration_ms_bucket{{job="genai-canary"}}[{window}]))',
    )
    baseline_p99 = fetch_prometheus_scalar(
        args.prom_url,
        f'histogram_quantile(0.99, rate(http_request_duration_ms_bucket{{job="genai-stable"}}[{window}]))',
    )
    print(f"p99 latency - canary: {canary_p99:.0f}ms  baseline: {baseline_p99:.0f}ms")
    if canary_p99 > args.latency_p99_threshold_ms:
        failures.append(
            f"P99 LATENCY exceeded: {canary_p99:.0f}ms > {args.latency_p99_threshold_ms:.0f}ms"
        )

    if failures:
        print("\nCANARY FAILED - rollback recommended:")
        for f in failures:
            print(f"  - {f}")
        sys.exit(1)
    else:
        print("\nCanary validation PASSED.")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
The following script performs the Stage 2 config parity check between staging and production:

A mid-size fintech team shipping an AI document-review service ran a deploy that updated the underlying model from version A to version B. Smoke tests and staging parity passed. The integration suite caught that the new model returned JSON with a slightly different key name for one field type — a change in output schema that would have broken the downstream parser for 12% of document types. The fix took 20 minutes; discovering the same bug after full rollout would have required a hotfix, a rollback, and customer notifications. The canary stage, run after the fix, showed p99 latency 8% lower for version B — a metric the team included in their launch announcement.
Running smoke tests against staging instead of prod
— the purpose of Stage 1 is to confirm the production deployment succeeded. Tests running against staging tell you nothing about the new prod pods.
Using exact-match assertions on model output

— GenAI output is non-deterministic; exact-match tests will flake. Use structural assertions (response is valid JSON, required fields present, content length in expected range) or model-graded quality checks for semantic validation.
Starting the canary before integration tests pass
— routing real users to a version with known integration failures means real users hit known bugs. The pipeline must be strictly sequential.
Treating the canary as a formality
— a 30-second soak time or a 50% canary split misses the point. The soak must be long enough for meaningful traffic and the split small enough to bound user impact if the canary is bad.
— comparing canary error rate against zero is useless if your service has a known 0.5% baseline error rate. Always compare against a rolling window of the current stable version.
Skipping the parity check when only code changed
— environment drift accumulates incrementally. A feature flag toggled manually in prod two weeks ago will only surface during a parity check, not from code inspection.

— assign a named team to each stage. SRE owns Stage 1, Platform Engineering owns Stage 2, Engineering and QA own Stage 3, SRE and PM own Stage 4. Ownership prevents situations where everyone assumes someone else will catch the problem.
Make the Definition of Done machine-checkable
— every criterion in the DoD should be a boolean that CI can evaluate, not a subjective judgment. "Integration suite 100% pass" is checkable; "looks good" is not.
— the smoke test suite and canary thresholds should live in version control alongside the service code, not as manual runbook steps. This ensures the verification evolves with the service.
— write down exact thresholds (error rate delta > 10%, p99 > 4 s, cost per request > 2x baseline) in the launch ticket before the deploy starts. Deciding under pressure produces inconsistent results.
— maintain a library of known-bad inputs (prompt injections, jailbreak attempts, malformed tool call requests) and assert that the output filter catches them. This library grows with every incident.

For high-volume GenAI services the canary validation script must query metrics from a time-series database rather than computing them in real time from request logs. At 10,000 requests per minute, log-based computation would be too slow. The Prometheus queries above are designed to run as instant queries against pre-computed recording rules, keeping the validation script response time under one second regardless of traffic volume.
The smoke test suite should run tests in parallel where they are independent. Health endpoint, model probe, and DB check can all run simultaneously. Use
to parallelize and keep Stage 1 under 60 seconds even as the test count grows. Add
to enforce per-test time limits so a hung model call cannot block the entire pipeline indefinitely.
enforces per-test time limits to prevent a hung model call from blocking the pipeline indefinitely.
provides p99 latency from histograms without storing every individual request duration.
— Kubernetes-native canary controllers that automate traffic splitting and metric-based rollback. They implement the canary soak loop as a CRD, removing the need for a custom script.
— feature flag platforms useful for the parity check: staging and prod flag states can be compared via their API rather than by diffing environment variables.
— host the four-stage pipeline as sequential CI jobs with
dependencies, ensuring strict ordering and automatic job cancellation on failure.
Deploy failures, missing secrets, dead dependencies
Regression in known scenarios, SLO violations, cost overruns
Unknown real-world inputs, tail-latency, emergent cost
Output schema, safety filters, token cost caps
Real prompt distribution, unexpected model behavior

The smoke test suite must never include real user data, even anonymized. Use purpose-built synthetic probe accounts with tokens that carry minimal permissions — read-only health, single-call inference quota. The canary stage routes live user traffic, so any failure in the new version must be isolated: ensure the canary pod has its own circuit-breaker configuration so a hung model call in the canary does not exhaust the connection pool shared with stable pods.

The config parity checker must handle secrets correctly by comparing secret references (for example the ARN of an AWS Secrets Manager secret) rather than the actual secret values. Logging actual secret values in CI output is a common misconfiguration that turns a verification script into a credential leak.

For rollback reliability, the previous stable version must remain deployed and receiving traffic on a separate target group for the entire duration of the canary soak. This means any rollback is a traffic-weight change — instant, no new deploy — rather than a re-deploy of the old artifact, which takes minutes and is itself subject to failure.
Pre-launch verification for GenAI services requires four sequential stages — smoke tests, staging parity, integration suite, canary soak — each with a binary gate that stops the pipeline on failure.
Smoke tests run against production immediately after deploy and confirm the deployment succeeded, not that the code is correct.
Staging parity is an active check, not an assumption — config drift between staging and production is the source of a large fraction of production-only failures.
The canary soak is the only stage that uses real user traffic; it catches failure modes that synthetic tests cannot reproduce, including unexpected prompt distributions and emergent cost patterns.
Rollback triggers must be quantitative and pre-agreed — error rate delta, p99 latency absolute threshold, cost per request ratio — decided before launch day, not during a live incident.
Integration test assertions on GenAI output must be structural or model-graded, never exact-match, to avoid chronic false failures from non-determinism.
The entire verification pipeline should live in version control alongside the service and run automatically in CI, ensuring it evolves with the service rather than becoming stale documentation.
